contents: Objective: To assess the impact of the multidisciplinary team in the management of the diabetic foot compared with those who did not receive multidisciplinary care. Method: A systematic review of the literature was conducted using the following databases: Cumulative Index to Nursing and Allied Health Literature (CINAHL), Medline, Embase and Cochrane Library. The following search terms were used: diabetic foot, multidisciplinary team, patient care team, multidisciplinary care team. Data were extracted using a bespoke data extraction tool and quality appraisal of the studies was undertaken using the EBL Critical Appraisal checklist. Data analysis was undertaken using RevMan with results presented as odds ratio for dichotomous data, or mean difference for continuous data, all with the associated 95% confidence intervals.Results: The search identified 19 eligible studies. Severity of amputation, death rates and length of hospital stay of clients receiving multidisciplinary team care were improved when compared with those who did not receive multidisciplinary team care. Ulcer healing and quality of life showed an improvement but not all studies explored these outcomes. Only 7 of the 19 articles appraised were found to be of acceptable quality, questioning the generalisability of the results. Conclusion: From the currently available evidence a positive impact of the multidisciplinary team on diabetic foot outcomes can be seen, but due to the lack of high-quality evidence and substantial heterogeneity in the studies, these results should be interpreted with caution. Declaration of interest: The authors have no conflicts of interest.

mong the many complications that can occur due to diabetes, foot ulceration is a leading problem.1 Diabetic foot ulcers (DFUs) occur due to various risk factors associated with diabetes such as sensory neuropathy, peripheral ischaemia and foot deformities.2 The presence of any of these factors can result in complications that can progress rapidly.3 When we look at Ireland, for example, where the authors of this review are located, the prevalence of people over 20  years of age living with diabetes in 2005 was estimated to be 4.7% and this figure was predicted to rise to 5.0% in 2015.4 Within this group it is estimated that there are between 20,470 and 41,020 episodes of diabetic foot ulceration in Ireland at any one time.5 However, this is not just a national problem isolated to Ireland, but rather a global issue. For example, in some islands in the Caribbean, prevalence rates of diabetes are 10-20%.6 Furthermore, in the UK one in 20 adults is thought to have diabetes, with UK prevalence rates estimated at 4.45%.7 diabetic foot * amputation * multidisciplinary team * ulceration * DFUsTreatment of diabetic foot disease has been found to

be the most costly of all diabetic complications.8 For example, the estimated cost of treating one client as an inpatient with a foot ulcer in Ireland is EUR30,000.8 In addition to monetary cost, foot disease is a physical disability and has a huge impact on a person's quality of life.9 Furthermore, a high risk of limb amputation is associated with the condition, with over 85% of amputations occurring in individuals with active or previous DFUs.6 Amputation is such a huge problem that it has been reported that a limb is lost to diabetes somewhere in the world every 30 seconds, a statistic that can be lowered significantly if appropriate approaches are provided.10 A key element suggested in the prevention of diabetic foot disease is multidisciplinary team (MDT) involvement in client care.11,12 An MDT is considered to be a pinnacle part of diabetic foot care and should consist of a group of health-care professionals each with knowledge of different aspects of the diabetic foot, collaborating together.13The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) has developed guidelines on the prevention and management of the diabetic foot.14 The need for a person with diabetes to be referred to an MDT is repeated throughout these guidelines at all stages of disease progression and for the prevention of the disease. However, the structure and delivery of these teams varies widely across health-care settings.14,15 In addition, in England it is reported that around one-fifth The impact of the multidisciplinary team in the management of individuals with diabetic foot ulcers: a systematic review

*A. Buggy,1 MSc (Wound Management and Tissue Viability), PG Dip, Professional Certificate (Community Nursing), RGN, CNM 2 Integrated Case Manager for Older Persons;  Z. Moore,1 PhD, Msc (leadership in Health Professions Education) MSc (Wound Healing and Tissue Repair), FFNMRCSI, PG Dip, Dip First Line Management, RGN, Professor and Head of the School of Nursing and Midwifery Corresponding author e-mail: Royal College of Surgeons in Ireland, Dublin, Ireland. educationof hospitals that provide diabetes care have no MDT diabetic foot service.16 The purpose of this systematic review was to explore the delivery of a diabetic foot MDT in greater detail and to identify if its existence leads to more positive diabetic foot outcomes. Thus, this systematic review explored the following question: What is the impact of the MDT in the management of individuals with DFUs? MethodsPICOUsing the PICO model, the participants of concern were adults aged 18 years or older with type 1 or type 2 diabetes mellitus, cared for in any health-care setting, with an active foot ulcer of any stage. The intervention of interest was the delivery of DFU treatment with an MDT approach, whereupon, the team had to be multidisciplinary health professionals. The comparison of interest was the delivery of care to manage people with DFUs by an individual health professional or using an unstructured, non-collaborative care model. The primary outcome measures were amputation rates, ulcer development and wound healing. With regard to amputation rates this included the level of amputation. Secondary outcome measures were: patients' experiences, mortality and resource use, which included length of hospital stay and cost of care. All English-language studies employing the following designs were included: randomised control trials, controlled clinical trials and comparative pre and post studies.

Search strategyThe following databases were searched over a period from 19 January 2016 to 21 January 2016: Cumulative Index to Nursing and Allied Health Literature (CINAHL), Medline, Embase and Cochrane Library. The following search terms were used: diabetic foot, multidisciplinary team, patient care team, multidisciplinary care team. The variation in terms was determined by the MeSH headings for each individual database. No limit was placed on year of publication. A limitation of English language only was placed on each search. The reference lists of chosen articles were screened but no additional articles were obtained from this screening. Data collectionPotentially eligible studies were selected by screening titles and abstracts, independently, by two reviewers. Full copies of potentially eligible studies were then obtained and read. Articles were then selected independently, by two reviewers, by applying the predefined inclusion and exclusion criteria. Data extraction Further articles were excluded at the data-extraction stage if they did not report on the outcomes required. The data from the included articles were extracted by two reviewers. The information extracted consisted of the first author, study title, year of publication, country where study was carried out, study design, care setting, sample size and findings. Data analysis and synthesisWhere suitable studies were analysed using Revman 5.3.17 The results of the remaining studies were analysed descriptively. Results are presented as odds ratio (OR) for dichotomous data with the associated 95% StudyReason for exclusionThewjitcharoen et al. (2014)20No comparison. Results for continuous data are presented as mean difference (MD) with the associated 95%. Statistical significance was set at p<=0.05. Where appropriate, a meta-analysis was undertaken of the results. Where meta-analysis was not possible, a narrative synthesis of the studies was undertaken consisting of a summary of each study including an outline of the author, sample size, results and a synopsis of these results.Quality appraisal Quality appraisal was undertaken using the EBL Critical Appraisal checklist.18 For systematic reviews the EBL checklist was not suitable so the PRISMA 2009 Checklist was employed.19 Each stage of the research process was validated, independently, by a second reviewer. In the case of disagreement, the plan was for consensus to be reached through discussion.

ResultsThe results from the literature search are shown in the PRISMA flow chart (Fig 1). A total of 537 articles were obtained for screening. Duplicates were then removed leaving a total of 424 articles. The titles and abstracts of the remaining articles were screened. Following this, 31 articles remained for further screening, ten articles of which were excluded (Table 1), leaving 21 articles.20-30 A further two were excluded at the data extraction stage as they did not fit the inclusion criteria (Table 1). From this process 19 articles remained and these formed the basis of the review. Geographical location The geographical location of the studies varied, three were conducted in Turkey,31-33 two in Taiwan,34,35 two in Spain,36,37 two in the US,38,39 one in the UK,40 one in Singapore,41 one in Belgium,42 one in Thailand,43 one in Lithuania,44 one in Ireland,45 one in Israel,46 one in Denmark,47 and one in Sweden.48  There was one systematic review.49Care settingAll studies were based in a hospital care setting. The majority focused on an acute care setting.31-33,35-36,38-43,45-48 Three focused on an outpatient department or hospital-based clinic,34,44,49

and one looked at data from both an acute and outpatient department setting.37 Participants and sample sizeAll participants had a diagnosis of diabetes with complications affecting the feet. This included past and/or present foot ulceration. In one study data for diabetic patients and non-diabetic patients who attended the MDT was provided.36 These were presented separately for the diabetic participants and so this study was eligible for inclusion.36 The smallest reported sample size was 66.32 No mean sample size could be obtained as some studies did not report their full sample size,35,36,39,40 or only reported the experimental sample size and not that of the control.45Study designs The studies' designs included 11 retrospective studies,31-32,35-40,42,45-46,48 4 prospective studies,33,40,41,44 two controlled clinical trials,43,47 one was a randomised control trial34  and one a systematic review.49 Interventions and follow-upAll studies evaluated the effect of an MDT on diabetic foot care compared with no MDT. One study looked at care before a team was established, after a team was established and then also included later data from when this MDT had commenced a foot clinic.37 Each study had a different combination of members in the team or did not list the exact members. The reported makeup of each team can be found in Table 2. Study MDT members Armstrong et al. (2012)39 Not listedAskoy et al. (2004)32Not listed

All rights reserved.educationQuality appraisal of included studiesQuality appraisal was carried out on 18 of the studies using the EBL critical appraisal checklist.18 Using this checklist, the study is shown to be valid if the proportion of 'yes' answers is >=75%, or if 'no' or 'unclear' answers are <=25%. The overall validities are presented in Table 3. The mean overall score was 71%, with the lowest being 58%40 and the highest being 88%.34 For the included systematic review49 the PRISMA checklist, which contains a list of 27 prompts, was used for quality assessment. These prompts are considered to be critical for the development of a transparent and valid systematic review.51 Analysing the systematic review we found an answer of yes to 12 questions and no to 15 meaning there are flaws in their reporting and in some incidences a lack of clarity. Primary outcomes amputation ratesAmputation rates were reported in all of the studies. This incorporated either one or a combination of total amputations, major amputations, minor amputations and re-amputation rates. Meta-analysis could not be undertaken for total amputation rates due to a substantial heterogeneity (I-squared=69%, p=0.007). If I-squared is >30%, or there is a low p-value (<0.10) in the chi-squared test for heterogeneity then it is not appropriate to undertake meta-analysis.50 Therefore total amputation rates had to be analysed separately with a combination of statistical and narrative analyses. RevMan analysis of one study showed a statistically

significant difference in favour of the experiment group when compared with the control group.35 Those in the experimental group were almost five times less likely to have a lower extremity amputation than those in the control group (MD-4.97, 95% CI -5.21--4.73; A further three studies found a significant statistical difference in favour of the experimental group when comparing total amputation rates pre and post MDT care,34,37,38 [OR 0.35; 95% CI: 0.23-0.53; p<0.00001]34 (Fig 3), [OR 0.35; 95% CI: 0.21-0.60; p=0.0001]38 (Fig 4), [OR 0.65; 95% CI: 0.47-0.90; p=0.01037] (Fig 5). RevMan analyses of four further studies did not identify statistically significant differences in total amputation rates between the control and experimental groups32,33,43,46 [OR 1.86;95% CI: 0.34-10.12; p=0.47]43 (Fig 6), [OR 1.12; 95% CI: 0.62-2.03; p=0.71]32 (Fig 7), [OR 0.60;95% CI: 0.34-1.06; p=0.08]46 (Fig 8), [OR 0.85; 95% CI 0.58-1.26; p=0.43]33 (Fig 9).For those studies that were analysed narratively, differences between MDT care and no MDT care were also seen. Of these one study reported a 36.7% and 39.4% total amputation rate before the MDT was established and an amputation rate of 21.1% after the team was established.31 Another study found a statistically significant difference (p<0.001) when analysing amputation rates before an MDT, after an MDT was established and after this MDT commenced a foot clinic.37 The study reports a 60.8% amputation rate in the control period, 50.2% amputations post MDT establishment and 42.8% amputations after this MDT commenced a foot clinic.

A high to low amputation ratio of 0.35 to 0.27 was reported in a further study, arising due to an increase in low level midfoot amputations (8.2% versus 26.1%, p<0.0001).39 A statistically significant difference was documented in one study, in favour of the experimental group with 17 amputations documented pre the MDT and 10 post (p=0.040).42 A final study found no statistically significant differences when evaluating total amputation rate pre and post an MDT establishment with 4.0 [95% CI: 2.6-5.5] pre MDT and 6.1 [95% CI: 4.9-7.2] post MDT.36 No sample sizes were given for this study in the text.Another study reported that the total number of annual amputations decreased by 49% after establishing a diabetic MDT.48 This study did not separate sample size for the control and experiment. Finally, the included systematic review reports a statistically significant decrease in amputation rates in five out of the seven included studies when an MDT approach was compared with standard care.49Primary outcomes: major and minor amputationsOf the studies nine reported major amputations,33,37,40-42,44-46 and eight minor amputations.33,36,37,40,44-47 Meta-analysis of the results of five of these studies for major amputations, found that there was a 37% reduction in the odds of having an amputation in the experimental group compared with the control group [OR .63; 95% CI: 0.48-0.82; p=0.0006) (Fig 10).42,46,44,47,37,33The studies that were not suitable for the Study author (date)EBL score %Alexandrescu et al. In one other study a total of eight major amputations were seen in the control and three in the experiment group.45 No specific figures were given and further analysis of this data could not be undertaken as no sample size was given for the control group. Similarly in one other study, 41 major amputations took place in the control group and 27 in the experimental group after an MDT was established.40 However, no sample size was provided so no further anaysis was appropriate. Another study reported that major amputations decreased from 78% previous to the MDT to 16.1% after its implementation (3.6/100000 inhabitants, p=0.001).48Meta-analysis of the data from four studies, pertaining Total events3491Heterogeneity: Not applicableFavours [experimental]Favours [control]

All rights reserved.educationto minor amputations, did not find a statistically significant difference between the study groups [OR 0.96, 95% CI 0.75 to 1.22; p=0.73], (Fig 11).33,37,44,46 One study that was not suitable for the meta-analysis reported an increase in minor amputations from 81 to 101 when an MDT was established,40 however, no sample size was provided for this study. A further study noted no change in minor amputation rates, where four below-knee amputations were performed in both the control and experimental groups.45 Similarly, in an additional study 56 minor amputations were performed in the control group and 44 for the experiment.47 Further analysis using RevMan was not possible as the events for the experimental group exceeded the sample size of 28, meaning some participants had more than one minor amputation. A further study reported that minor amputations increased from 28% before the MDT to 53%.48 Only one study noted a statistically significant difference in minor amputation rate, in favour of the experimental group;36 intervention, 5.0 (2.3-7.8), control, 5.7 (3.9-7.5) p=0.732.Primary outcomes: re-amputation Re-amputation rate was documented in two studies.34,48

In both a statistically significant difference was seen in favour of the experimental group. A 59% reduction in the odds of having a re-amputation was found in one study [OR 0.41; 95% CI 0.20 to 0.85; p=0.02] (Fig 12),34 and a decrease from 36% to 22% (p<0.05) was reported in the second, however, the specific data were not presented in the study.48 One study also provided data for Syme amputations (an amputation through the ankle joint), trans-metatarsal, and ray and trans-phalangeal amputations32 Syme amputations accounted for 7.4% of amputations in the experimental group and 8% in the control. Trans-phalangeal accounted for 25.9% of the amputations in the experimental group and 0% in the control. Non-specified amputations classified as 'others', were not undertaken in the experimental group, however, they were undertaken in 5% of cases within the control group. Primary outcomes: ulcer healingUlcer healing was documented differently in the studies. Recurrent foot ulcer rate at a two-year follow-up appointment was assessed in one study which found a statistically significant difference  before and after.44 There was a 69% reduction in the odds of having a recurrent foot ulcer in the experimental group compared with the control group [OR 0.31; 95% CI 0.15-0.63; p=0.001] (Fig 13). All rights reserved.educationA further study looked at ulcers that healed without requiring amputation in addition to the rate of unhealed ulcers.33 RevMan analysis found no statistically significant difference between the groups [OR 1.30; 95% CI: 0.88-1.91, p=0.18] (Fig 14) and [OR 0.82; 95% CI: 0.48-1.39, p=0.45] (Fig 15).Secondary outcomes: length of stay in hospitalMeta-analysis for outcomes related to length of hospital stay could not be undertaken due to a substantial heterogeneity among the studies (I-squared=96%). The studies were, therefore, analysed individually using RevMan. Two studies showed statistically significant differences in length of hospital stay in favour of the experimental groups ([MD -5.80 95% CI: -7.69- -3.91, p<0.00001]33 (Fig 16), [MD -12.48 95% CI: -17.62--7.34, p <0.00001]34 (Fig 17).

In two other studies there was statistically significant differences observed between the study groups and length of stay in hospital, [OR 0.20, 95% CI -0.47- 0.87; p=0.56;37 OR 0.80; 95% CI: 0.42-1.53, p=0.50]46 (Fig 18 and 19).Length of hospital stay was reported in one study as 12 days for the experiment and 15 days for the control.45 The authors also report a reduction in admissions from 131 people in the control group to 120 people in the experiment group. A statistically significant difference in length of hospital stay, in favour of the experimental group, was reported in another study (p=0.0005).41 This study presented the average length of hospital days per year of the study, before team formation in 2002, after team formation, 2003-2007. The results were as follows; 2002, 20.36, 2003, 19.03, 2004, 13.74, 2005, 10.81, 2006, 11.67, 2007, 12.20. This study also reports a non-statistically significant difference in the readmission rate among the study groups (p>0.05), with readmission percentage rate reported for each year of the study as: 2002, 13.11%, 2003, 7.14%, 2004, 6.76%, 2005, 7.22%, ExperimentalControlOdds ratioOdds ratioStudy or subgroupEventsTotalEventsTotalWeightM-H, Fixed, 95% ClM-H, Fixed, 95% ClHeterogeneity: Not applicableFavours [experimental]Favours [control]ExperimentalControlOdds ratioOdds ratioStudy or subgroupEventsTotalEventsTotalWeightM-H, Fixed, 95% ClM-H, Fixed, 95% ClTotal events1128Heterogeneity: Not applicableFavours [experimental]Favours [control]ExperimentalControlOdds ratioOdds ratioStudy or subgroupEventsTotalEventsTotalWeightM-H, Fixed, 95% ClM-H, Fixed, 95% Cl

A further study looked at average length of hospital stay days between the years 2004 and 2009.40 The MDT was established in 2006. The results presented from 2004 to 2009 were: 2004, 16, 2005, 18, 2006, 17, 2007, 13, 2008, 14, and 2009, 15.5 days, respectively. Secondary outcomes: hospitalisation One study reported on the number of diabetics that required hospitalisation and found a statistically significant difference between the groups48 [OR 0.16; 95% CI: 0.11-0.24, p=0.00001] (Fig 20).48 This shows that there was a 69% reduction in the odds of the experimental group being admitted into hospital. Secondary outcomes: cost of care The cost of care was reported in three studies.41,43,45 A statistically significant difference was reported in the first study in favour of the experimental group, which found a difference in cost of US$1127.02 for those involved with the MDT foot clinic versus $1824.58 for those receiving standard care (p<0.02).43ExperimentalControlMean differenceMean differenceStudy or subgroupMeanSDTotalMeanSDTotalWeightIV, Fixed, 95% ClIV, Fixed, 95% ClHeterogeneity: Not applicableFavours [experimental]Favours [control]ExperimentalControlMean differenceMean differenceStudy or subgroupMeanSDTotalMeanSDTotalWeightIV, Fixed, 95% ClIV, Fixed, 95% Cl

All rights reserved.educationThe second study found a non-statistically significant difference between the groups (p>0.05).41 This study reported a yearly mean cost per patient as follows: 2002, $8847.17, 2003, $9935.59, 2004, $7659.55, 2005, $6195.77, 2006, $6320.19, 2007, $8383.79. The MDT in the study formed in 2003. Cost is reported in the third study as EUR2,465,766 for the control and EUR2,237,641 for the experiment group.45 However, the sample size for the control group was not reported so more detailed analysis of these figures was not possible. Secondary outcomes: quality of life Patients' experiences were explored in one study using a quality of life questionnaire.43 The results showed a statistically significant difference between the groups, with there being an increase in quality of life found in the experimental group [MD: 8.70; 95% CI 1.07-16.33; p=0.03] (Fig 21)Secondary outcomes: mortality Inpatient participant deaths were assessed in two studies.37,46 When analysed together there was a statistically significant difference between the groups [OR .54; 95% CI: 0.27 to 1.07, p=0.08]. This shows that there is a 46% reduction in the odds of a participant in the experimental group dying (Fig 22.).

DiscussionThe multidisciplinary teamThe disciplines that made up the MDT differed throughout the articles. The majority contained a variation of consultants, nurse specialists, podiatrists and orthotics. This is comparable with the National Diabetes Programme guidance on the model of care for the diabetic foot, which recommends that diabetic foot care teams should consist of consultants, diabetic nurse specialists and podiatrists.52 Some of the studies did not discuss the makeup of the MDT,31,32,34,36,43 leaving it unclear whether the team consisted of the appropriate set of members as recommended in the guidelines. Also, one study described two consultants, orthopaedic and endocrinology, coming together to form a MDT and six months later including a nurse.46 It could be argued that with two consultants this was not a MDT and even when the nurse was included it did not make up the best recommended team.13,52  The large variation in the makeup of the MDT seen throughout the included studies leaves it unclear as to if the appropriate health-care professionals were collaborating in all cases (Table 2).52 In addition, it makes it difficult to compare the results of the 19 studies with each other to draw conclusions when no team had the exact same set of skills. The delivery settings of the teams were predominately hospital-based inpatient but some also included outpatient clinics. It has previously been documented that the setting can vary once the appropriate health professionals are collaborating and sharing the care.13 Research containing similar or identical team make-ups would help to further determine if it is in fact the MDT that has an impact on diabetic foot care or if it is the collective efforts of two or more disciplines that works.ExperimentalControlOdds ratioOdds ratioStudy or subgroupEventsTotalEventsTotalWeightM-H, Fixed, 95% ClM-H, Fixed, 95% ClTotal events2426

All rights reserved.educationThe effect of the MDT on amputation ratesThe ultimate aim in the care of the diabetic foot is to avoid ulceration and resulting amputation.9 This concept was addressed in all 19 of the included studies. Much of the data were collected by analysing p a t i e n t s '  n o t e s  o r  d a t a b a s e s , retrospectively.31,32,35-39,42,46,48 This must be recognised as a limitation of these studies as they were relying on the relevant documentation for analysis having been completed accurately. Furthermore, there is a risk that all amputations recorded may not be done so in a way that allows for a collective analysis. This was obvious in some of the studies, given the substantial heterogeneity noted when assessing suitability for meta-analysis.Total amputations were addressed in 15 of the studies. Of these studies nine showed positive results and six showed negative results. However, when these results were analysed two of the studies that showed positive results and two negative results, others failed to report separate control and experiment sample sizes, which weakens the interpretation of these results.31,36,39,48 In addition, the included systematic review, which showed a positive impact of MDTs, did not provide any figures or data to demonstrate where the results came from, making these results impossible to analyse.49 It has been documented that when effective measures

are put in place the severity of a diabetic foot complication can be reduced.53 Major amputation results were reported in nine studies. When the results of both meta-analyses and narrative analysis were explored, all reported a statistically significant reduction in major amputations after the implementation of an MDT. However, again, two of these did not report a clear sample size leaving the relevance of those results unclear.40,45Minor amputation results were reported in nine studies; seven of the studies favoured the control group and one showed no change in amputation rate.45 Only one of the nine studies found a statistically significant reduction in minor amputations in the experiment group. On the surface this may appear to be a negative impact of the MDT but it could be argued that this was a favourable outcome as the results would indicate the severity of amputation decreases once an individual is under the care of an MDT. However, what is meant by major and minor amputations is not explained in the studies so what they entail must just be presumed. Only one study divided the results into very specific amputations.32 More studies like this would be beneficial to understand the precise impact on amputation severity.

Re-amputation rates were reduced in both the studies reporting this outcome.34,48 However, neither study reports on the follow-up care after the initial amputation. It was noted that early intervention and the type of surgery performed was a contributing factor to lower re-amputation rates in the experiment group in one study.34 The other does not place much emphasis on this in the results as some of the ulcers followed in the study had yet to heal and potentially could result in re-amputation at a later time.48 Therefore, for the purpose of this review no conclusion can be made regarding the impact of the MDT on re-amputation rates. Use for licensed purposes only. No other uses without permission. All rights reserved.educationMDT and ulcer healingUlcer healing is also cited as a primary goal of the diabetic MDT.9 This was only explored in two of the included studies.33,44 Although one of these studies found a statistically significant difference in favour of the experimental group,44 no firm conclusions can be made in relation to ulcer healing due to the lack of research in this area. More robust research in the area of ulcer healing, progression and in fact also ulcer prevention--which was not documented in any study--is essential to determine the impact of the multidisciplinary foot care team on this primary goal. MDT and resource use. The treatment of diabetic foot disease is costly and often results in frequent hospitalisation.52 Hospitalisation was reported in seven studies. A reduction in hospital stay was found in five of these studies.33,34,41,45,48 Results such as these would imply a substantial saving in the cost of treatment as inpatient time is reduced. Indeed, of the three studies that looked at cost of treatment, all three found that savings were made when MDT care was introduced.41,43,45 However, the evidence was limited as it was only presented in three studies. More studies that explore resource use to weigh up the impact of it as a collective would help to interpret the significance of resource use in this area better. MDT and quality of lifeDiabetic foot complications affect people both physically and mentally and therefore have a negative impact on a person's quality of life.15 Quality of life was researched in just one of the included studies.43 Although this study found the MDT had a positive impact on participants' quality of life the authors themselves reported that the questionnaire used was not without its flaws as it asked participants to draw on past events, which may have resulted in some recall bias. With only one study reporting on quality of life it is

impossible to draw firm conclusions from these results. With best practice guidelines advocating that the patient should be at the centre of their care and involved throughout their wound management process this is surprising.5,54 Indeed, if the patient should be placed at the centre of the care plan, then it is reasonable to suggest that they should be included as a member of the team. However, none of the studies included in this review echoed this sentiment. Furthermore, a literature review that sought to explore the concept of MDTs found that the client was included as a member of the team in only 1% of the literature reviewed.55 It would, in turn, be beneficial to see whether quality of life improves when patients are more involved in their care. It is evident, however, that further research is needed into the outcomes of diabetic foot ulceration when the service user is considered as an equal member of the team. MDT and mortalityMortality is a serious complication of diabetic foot

ulceration and is a risk to all clients with diabetic foot disease regardless of its severity.56 A reduction in mortality with those who were involved in MDT care was found in the two studies that addressed this outcome.37,46 However, a breakdown of cause of death was not given, which would have been beneficial to understand the significance of this mortality rate better. Also, as only two studies reported on mortality the data are limited. Future studies in this area should strive to produce data in relation to mortality and MDT care. Quality of the evidence Although improvements were seen in many of the outcomes post MDT care these results should be interpreted with caution. Following quality appraisal, 61% of studies included were found not to reach the required validity scores. This arose mainly due to insufficient reporting within the studies, making it many aspects of the published studies unclear. For example, many of the studies failed to give clear sample sizes. This left it impossible to interpret the results properly or to perform further statistical analysis. A further concerning point is that only one study declared that the researchers played no part in care delivery. If the researchers in any of the studies were also members of the MDT this would introduce a strong element of bias to the studies.18

Within this systematic review, many of the study's results were reviewed individually rather than through a meta-analysis. This was due to a substantial heterogeneity in many cases. Meta-analysis can only be performed when studies are similar to each other.57 Furthermore, as the majority of studies were based in a hospital setting and no one study had the exact same team makeup it is difficult to generalise the results into the wider clinical setting. The evidence presented cannot be used to firmly guide future practice. From the currently available evidence, an impact of the MDT on diabetic foot outcomes can be seen but due to the lack of high-quality evidence and substantial heterogeneity these results should be interpreted with caution. ConclusionThis systematic review aimed to explore the impact of the MDT on the management of individuals with DFUs. A comprehensive review of current evidence did find some positive effects of the MDT on DFUs, namely; amputation rate, severity of amputation and resource use. MDT care also appears to improve mortality and quality of life of people with this condition. However, due to methodological heterogeneity and some issues around methodological Reflective Questions *What is the significance of diabetic foot disease for the individual, the health service and society as a whole? *Why is a multidisciplinary approach to management of the diabetic foot ulcers important? *Why is the assessment of study quality important in determining the significance of the study results?(c) 2017 MA Healthcare ltd

(c) MA Healthcare Ltd. Downloaded from magonlinelibrary.com by 155.198.030.043 on June 14, 2017.Use for licensed purposes only. No other uses without permission. All rights reserved.educationquality the outcomes of this review should be interpreted with caution. MDTs have been widely cited as an important strategy for managing the diabetic foot.11,12,14 Therefore, it is important that this conclusion is interpreted as a lack of high-quality evidence rather than evidence of no effect. The promising results found in many of the studies warrant further research. The main shortcomings of the studies included in this review are unclear reporting of sample sizes, risk of bias if the researchers were also the health professionals who were involved with the MDT, and the retrospective nature of many of the studies that may have resulted in inaccurate reporting of results. To facilitate further robust meta-analysis of the impact of diabetic foot teams, homogeneity of study design, characteristics and reporting is desirable. Addressing these issues should aim to enhance credibility of future results on this topic. JWC
